metadata.summary: Financial crime domain data validation and normalization library.

documentation:
# rigour

Data cleaning and validation functions for processing various types of text emanating and describing the business world. This applies to human and company names, language, territory
and country codes, corporate and tax identifiers, etc.

The underlying idea is that handling these sorts of descriptors is easy on first glance, but reveals a dizzying set of complexity when carried into production. This is why `rigour` consolidates implementations that have already met some edge cases and are well-tested.

## Installing `rigour`

You can just grab the library from PyPI:

```bash
pip install -U rigour
```

## Usage & documentation 

See: https://opensanctions.github.io/rigour/

functions.name: rigour.time.utc_now
documentation: Return the current datetime in UTC.

functions.name: rigour.time.naive_now
documentation: Return the current datetime as a naive datetime.

functions.name: rigour.time.utc_date
documentation: Return the current date in UTC.

functions.name: rigour.text.distance.dam_levenshtein
documentation:
Compute the Damerau-Levenshtein distance between two strings.

Args:
    left: A string.
    right: A string.

Returns:
    An integer of changed characters.

functions.name: rigour.text.distance.levenshtein
documentation:
Compute the Levenshtein distance between two strings.

Args:
    left: A string.
    right: A string.

Returns:
    An integer of changed characters.

functions.name: rigour.text.distance.levenshtein_similarity
documentation:
Compute the Damerau Levenshtein similarity of two strings. The similiarity is
the percentage distance measured against the length of the longest string.

Args:
    left: A string.
    right: A string.
    max_edits: The maximum number of edits allowed.
    max_percent: The maximum fraction of the shortest string that is allowed to be edited.

Returns:
    A float between 0.0 and 1.0.

functions.name: rigour.text.distance.is_levenshtein_plausible
documentation:
A sanity check to post-filter name matching results based on a budget
of allowed Levenshtein distance. This basically cuts off results where
the Jaro-Winkler or Metaphone comparison was too lenient.

Args:
    left: A string.
    right: A string.
    max_edits: The maximum number of edits allowed.
    max_percent: The maximum percentage of edits allowed.

Returns:
    A boolean.

functions.name: rigour.text.distance.jaro_winkler
documentation:
Compute the Jaro-Winkler similarity of two strings.

Args:
    left: A string.
    right: A string.

Returns:
    A float between 0.0 and 1.0.

functions.name: rigour.text.scripts.is_alpha
documentation:
Check if a character is alphabetic. This improves on the function implemented on
`str` by including characters for the whole unicode range.

functions.name: rigour.text.scripts.is_alphanum
documentation: Check if a character is alpha-numeric.

functions.name: rigour.text.scripts.is_modern_alphabet
documentation:
Check if a word is written in a modern alphabet. The term alphabet is
used in a narrow sense here: it includes only alphabets that have vowels and
are safely transliterated to latin. Basically: Cyrillic, Greek, Armenian,
and Latin.

functions.name: rigour.text.scripts.is_latin
documentation: Check if a word is written in the latin alphabet.

modules.name: rigour.mime
documentation:
``rigour.mime`` handles the parsing and normalisation of internet MIME types in
Python. This can be useful to normalise invalid, or misformatted MIME types
emitted by remote web servers.

## Usage

The simplest use is to normalise a MIME type:

```python
from rigour.mime import normalize_mimetype

assert normalize_mimetype('TEXT/PLAIN') == 'text/plain'
assert normalize_mimetype('plain/text') == 'text/plain'
assert normalize_mimetype(None) == 'application/octet-stream'
assert normalize_mimetype('') == 'application/octet-stream'
```

Internally, `rigour.mime` uses a `MIMEType` object to handle parsing. It can
be used to access more specific information, like human readable labels:

```python
from rigour.mime import parse_mimetype

parsed = parse_mimetype('text/plain')
assert parsed.family == 'text'
assert parsed.subtype == 'plain'
assert parsed.label == 'Plain text'
```

## Open issues

* Internationalisation, i.e. make the human-readable labels available in
  multiple languages.
* Expand replacements for specific MIME types.

This module is an inlined version of the `pantomime` library.

functions.name: rigour.addresses.cleaning.clean_address
documentation: Remove common formatting errors from addresses.

modules.name: rigour.addresses
documentation:
# Postal/location address handling

This set of helpers is designed to help with the processing of real-world
addresses, including composing an address from individual parts, and cleaning it up.

```python
import rigour.addresses as format_one_line

address = {
    "road": "Bahnhofstr.",
    "house_number": "10",
    "postcode": "86150",
    "city": "Augsburg",
    "state": "Bayern",
    "country": "Germany",
}
address_text = format_one_line(address, country="DE")
```

functions.name: rigour.addresses.format.format_address
documentation:
Format the given address part into a multi-line string that matches the
conventions of the country of the given address.

Args:
    address: The address parts to be combined. Common parts include:
        summary: A short description of the address.
        po_box: The PO box/mailbox number.
        street: The street or road name.
        house: The descriptive name of the house.
        house_number: The number of the house on the street.
        postal_code: The postal code or ZIP code.
        city: The city or town name.
        county: The county or district name.
        state: The state or province name.
        state_district: The state or province district name.
        state_code: The state or province code.
        country: The name of the country (words, not ISO code).
        country_code: A pre-normalized country code.
    country: ISO code for the country of the address.

Returns:
    A single-line string with the formatted address.

functions.name: rigour.addresses.format.format_address_line
documentation:
Format the given address part into a single-line string that matches the
conventions of the country of the given address.

Args:
    address: The address parts to be combined. Common parts include:
        summary: A short description of the address.
        po_box: The PO box/mailbox number.
        street: The street or road name.
        house: The descriptive name of the house.
        house_number: The number of the house on the street.
        postal_code: The postal code or ZIP code.
        city: The city or town name.
        county: The county or district name.
        state: The state or province name.
        state_district: The state or province district name.
        state_code: The state or province code.
        country: The name of the country (words, not ISO code).
        country_code: A pre-normalized country code.
    country: ISO code for the country of the address.

Returns:
    A single-line string with the formatted address.

methods.name: rigour.names.part.NamePart.__init__
documentation: __slots__ = ["name", "lower", "index", "metaphone", "is_alphabet", "ascii"]

A part of a name, such as a given name or family name. This 
object is used to compare and match names. It stores representations
of the name in various processing forms.

documentation: Determine the percentage of a string that's latin.

documentation: Pick the best name from a list of names, using a weighted levenshtein distances.

functions.name: rigour.names.pick.pick_name
documentation:
Pick the best name from a list of names. This is meant to pick a centroid
name, with a bias towards names in a latin script.

Args:
    names (List[str]): A list of names.

Returns:
    Optional[str]: The best name for display.

modules.name: rigour.langs.synonyms
documentation: This is a set of synonyms for pragmatic usage in NLP. It is based on
working with Tesseract 3.04, but should be applicable elsewhere.

functions.name: rigour.langs.synonyms.expand_synonyms
documentation: Expand a language code into a set of codes.

modules.name: rigour.langs
documentation:
# Language code handling

This library helps to normalise the ISO 639 codes used to describe languages from
two-letter codes to three letters, and vice versa.

```python
import rigour.langs as languagecodes

assert 'eng' == languagecodes.iso_639_alpha3('en')
assert 'eng' == languagecodes.iso_639_alpha3('ENG ')
assert 'en' == languagecodes.iso_639_alpha2('ENG ')
```

Uses data from: https://iso639-3.sil.org/
See also: https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php

functions.name: rigour.langs.iso_639_alpha3
documentation:
Convert a given language identifier into an ISO 639 Part 2 code, such
as "eng" or "deu". This will accept language codes in the two- or three-
letter format, and some language names. If the given string cannot be
converted, `None` will be returned.

```pycon
>>> iso_639_alpha3('en')
'eng'
```

functions.name: rigour.langs.iso_639_alpha2
documentation:
Convert a language identifier to an ISO 639 Part 1 code, such as "en"
or "de". For languages which do not have a two-letter identifier, or
invalid language codes, `None` will be returned.

functions.name: rigour.langs.list_to_alpha3
documentation:
Parse all the language codes in a given list into ISO 639 Part 2 codes
and optionally expand them with synonyms (i.e. other names for the same
language).

functions.name: rigour.urls.compare.compare_urls
documentation:
Compare two URLs and return a float between 0 and 1 representing the
similarity between them. Before comparison, clean both URLs in a destructive
way.

functions.name: rigour.urls.cleaning.build_url
documentation: Compose a URL with the given query parameters.

functions.name: rigour.urls.cleaning._clean_url
documentation:
Perform intensive care on URLs to make sure they have a scheme
and a host name. If no scheme is given HTTP is assumed.

functions.name: rigour.urls.cleaning.clean_url
documentation:
Perform intensive care on URLs to make sure they have a scheme
and a host name. If no scheme is given HTTP is assumed.
